key_insight: A neuropathology-confirmed benchmark found that a confidence-aware LLM system for neurological diagnosis matched or slightly outperformed neurologists on top-3 differential accuracy.
clinical_relevance: The study suggests AI could help support expert diagnostic reasoning in complex neurological cases, but performance should be judged against biological ground truth rather than clinician consensus alone.
method: Built NeuroBench from 203 retrospective Massachusetts General Hospital Brain Cutting Conference cases with autopsy-confirmed diagnoses.
Tested DIAGNO, an LLM-based diagnostic system using confidence-aware retrieval-augmented generation for lower-confidence cases.
Compared DIAGNO, neurologists, GPT-4o, and DeepSeek R1 using blinded adjudicators against neuropathological ground truth.
evidence_signal: On NeuroBench, DIAGNO reached 0.67 top-3 accuracy and 0.74 taxonomy-level accuracy, with stronger performance than neurologists in disagreement cases and high 90% top-3 concordance.
watch_item: This is a retrospective benchmark from a single-center autopsy cohort, so generalization to live clinical settings and prospective use still needs validation.
paper_title: Assessing AI and Neurologist Diagnostic Reasoning Against Neuropathological Ground Truth
